chunks.text:
[0:04] What's up geeks and welcome to the channel! Imagine you have a burgers restaurant and for
[0:11] this restaurant you created a delivery application that delivers burgers
[0:15] Now if each type of burger we deliver is represented by a class
[0:18] then the code backing the delivery and production of these burgers can be written as follows
[0:24] after receiving our client request our application will identify the food it needs to prepare
[0:29] and based on this information it will proceed by creating the
[0:32] object corresponding to this type of burger, prepare it, and then return it to the user
[0:38] however you see there's a slight limitation here as the BeefBurger and VeggieBurger objects you are
[0:43] creating represent different types of products and the method we are implementing here can
[0:48] only return a single type a single object therefore if these products do not have a
[0:53] common base class or interface we won't be able to proceed with such an implementation
[0:59] So let's create a base class for our products and try to use it within the code of our application

[1:08] two products into a single class, what we can also do is extract the
[1:13] common behaviors of these products to this class as they are already implementing them
[1:18] Okay our application looks complete now but when we see code like this
[1:21] we know that when things change like if we add new recipes to our delivery
[1:25] menu or more products to our restaurant we're going to have to open
[1:29] this code and change it and that violates our open-closed
[1:32] and single responsibility principles We'll also end up writing the same piece of
[1:37] code in several places in the app making things even worse
[1:40] So what do we do? Well let's look at what varies and encapsulate it!
[1:44] When our restaurant grows and changes over time we are going to add and remove recipes from the menu
[1:50] So in the second version of our app we removed the veggie burger and added the chicken burger option

[2:04] this code is going to remain open To fix this what we do is take our
[2:08] burger's creation code and encapsulate it, separate it into a class called a Factory.
[2:15] Why a factory? because this is a class whose sole responsibility is
[2:19] creating burgers, it's a burger factory. Now anytime we want to order something
[2:24] from our restaurant we'll call this method,
[2:27] pass it a type, and after figuring it out, the method will return the requested order
[2:32] for us that implements the Burger class Now all this code is in one place nicely
[2:37] separated from the rest of the restaurant code and to integrate it back in our original method
[2:42] what we have to do is create a factory object pass to it the type and boom our food is ready

[2:42] what we have to do is create a factory object pass to it the type and boom our food is ready
[2:48] our orderBurger method no longer has to worry about the concrete type of the burger requested
[2:53] because whatever it is we know that it will implement the Burger interface
[2:58] and that's all we care about. We call this design the Simple Factory Idiom
[3:03] and our example can be represented by the class diagram you see in front of you
[3:07] it starts with the client of the factory in our case the Restaurant class itself
[3:11] then we have our Factory which is the only place where the concrete type of burgers is known
[3:16] and then we have our concrete products, the burgers our restaurant
[3:20] delivers via the app we are implementing, all concrete products must implement the
[3:24] same interface or abstract product. Now, one thing to note is that the
[3:29] simple factory we've been discussing since the beginning of this video
[3:32] is not a full-fledged official pattern it is more of an idiom that's commonly used

[3:29] simple factory we've been discussing since the beginning of this video
[3:32] is not a full-fledged official pattern it is more of an idiom that's commonly used
[3:37] however it is the first step in understanding one of the most powerful design patterns out there:
[3:44] The Factory Method Design Pattern. This pattern is a creational design pattern,
[3:50] it loosens the coupling of a given code by separating the product's construction code
[3:55] from the code that uses this product. In our previous example the product
[3:59] was the burgers we are delivering. So let us go back to this example to
[4:02] try and see together why the implementation we did isn't considered a full-fledged design pattern.
[4:08] When we encapsulated the creation logic of our burger in the simple factory class
[4:13] we isolated this logic and created a class whose sole purpose is creating burger objects
[4:18] by doing this we followed the single responsibility principle and that's great!
[4:22] However if you look closely you will see that this class on its own is still open for modification
[4:28] and when we add more recipes to our menu we will have to add if-statements to this method

[4:58] the Restaurant class this method is the

[5:07] in this example and based on the burgers we are delivering these subclasses will
[5:10] be the BeefBurgerRestaurant and the VeggieBurgerRestaurant
[5:14] You see that is actually how you pinpoint the usage of a factory in a given code
[5:18] as the factory method design pattern relies heavily on inheritance

[7:20] but also and most importantly you will be following and applying the open-closed
[7:25] principle and the single responsibility principle as you are centralizing the product creation
